0 phases:  the prompt emission and the afterglow emis-

5

3
shallower) during initial ∼10 s (Nousek et al. 2006;
Liang et al. 2007). Although the origin of the shallow
decay is still  largely unknown,  it is generally believed
to be related to the central engine of GRBs.  Several

models are proposed to explain the shallow decay such
as the energy injection model (Zhang & M´esz´aros 2001;
Zhang et al. 2006; Granot & Kumar 2006), evolving mi-
crophysical parameters (Fan & Piran 2006; Panaitescu
et al. 2006; Ioka, K. et al. 2006), and the wind model
(Shen & Matzner 2011; Dereli-B´egu´e et al. 2022). While
a long activity of the central engine, such as a magne-
tar, has been considered for the energy injection model
(Zhang et al. 2006), Kusafuka & Asano (2024) show that
the finite thickness of the ejecta can naturally cause the
evolution of the forward shock equivalent to the energy
injection model. The wind model is also attractive. Un-
like the energy injection model,  it does not require a
long activity for the central engine with a non-trivial
evolution of the luminosity or bulk Lorentz factor (Shen
& Matzner 2011; Dereli-B´egu´e et al. 2022).
GeV–TeV gamma-ray observations can provide clues
to this enigmatic shallow decay phase. Recently Asano
(2024) showed that GeV–TeV observations can help to
determine the different models of shallow decay phase.
Dereli-B´egu´e et al.  (2022) showed that non-detection
of GeV photon can be used to test the wind model  if
synchrotron emission is considered, while Asano (2024)

2
suggests that  inverse Compton emission can produce
a bright GeV–TeV emission.  GeV afterglows  in ini-
3  4
tial  10 –10 s are detected by the Large Area Tele-
scope (LAT; Atwood et al. (2009)) on the Fermi space
telescope for a fraction of GRBs (Ajello et al. 2019).
However, Yamazaki et al. (2020) argued that GeV–TeV
GRBs have less noticeable shallow decay phase in the
early X-ray afterglow:  Even if  the GeV–TeV GRBs
have shallow decay phase, their decay indices tend to
be steeper than the typical value. Moreover, based on
the results from Ding et al. (2022), only 3% of their sam-
ple of the shallow decay GRBs have Eγ,iso larger than
54
10  erg.  This value is much lower than the fraction
54
of GRBs with just Eγ,iso > 10  erg, which is ∼ 24%
(Atteia et al. 2017).  Sharma et al. (2021) argued that
hyper-energetic GRBs with beam-corrected prompt en-
52
ergy greater than ∼ 10  erg show no shallow decay fea-
ture in their X-ray light curve. The authors also claimed
that the central engine of those hyper-energetic GRBs
is a black hole rather than a magnetar, since the total
burst energy would exceed the maximum possible rota-
tional energy of the magnetar that can be converted into
the GRB jet. These results might suggest that a black
hole is not related to the X-ray shallow decay.  How-
ever, some studies argue that the X-ray shallow decay is
mostly attributed to a black hole under an assumption
of isotropic energy release from a magnetar during the
shallow decay (e.g. Li et al. (2018)).
The detection of GRB 240529A was initially reported
by the Burst Alert Telescope (BAT; Barthelmy et al.
(2005)) on the Neil Gehrels Swift Observatory (Swift)
spacecraft on 29 May 2024, 02:58:31 UT (hereafter T0;
Eyles-Ferris et al. (2024)).

The prompt duration T90
(the time corresponding to 5%–95% of a burst fluence)
is 160.67 ± 14.52 s in the 15–350 keV band. The time-
averaged BAT spectrum is well modeled by a single
power-law model with photon index Γ = −1.68 ± 0.04,
suggesting a high peak energy above 150 keV. The
fluence in the 15–150 keV band is Sγ,15–150 = 2.1 ×
−5  −2
10  erg cm  (Markwardt et al. 2024).  The prompt
emission was also detected by the AstroSat  satellite
(Joshi et al. 2024),  the Hard X-ray Modulation Tele-
scope (Tan et al. 2024), Mars Odyssey (Kozyrev et al.
2024), and Konus-Wind (Svinkin et al. 2024).  There
was no onboard trigger of the prompt emission by the
Fermi Gamma-Ray Burst Monitor.
The X-Ray Telescope (XRT; Burrows et al. (2005))
on Swift started observations of GRB 240529A 107 s af-
ter BAT trigger (Dichiara et al. 2024). The XRT light
curve of GRB 240529A is presented in Figure 1. From
its light curve, the afterglow shows shallow decay ex-
pressed by a temporal index of −0.21±0.03, followed by

a steeper decay than usual cases with a temporal index
4
of −2.02±0.04 with a break at 1.63(5) ×10 s (see Sec-
tion 2.2 for the detail of the light curve study). 0.3–10
keV energy flux during the shallow decay phase is ap-
−10  −2 −1
proximately 2 × 10  erg cm  s  , which is one of the
brightest shallow decay in X-rays (Ding et al. 2022; Zhao
et al. 2019; Tang et al. 2019). There is a bump in the
X-ray lightcurve a few hundred seconds after the BAT
trigger. While this can be explained by an X-ray flare,
which is frequently seen in X-ray lightcurves (Chincarini
et al. 2007), Tian et al. (2025) proposes a strange star
model for this bump. We do not focus on this behavior
in this paper.
An optical counterpart whose the position is consis-
tent with the enhanced XRT position was detected by
the Swift/UVOT (Shilling et al. 2024), which was later
confirmed by other optical  telescopes (Kumar et al.
2024; Fu et al. 2024; Perez-Garcia et al. 2024; Dut-
ton et al. 2024; Mo et al. 2024; Odeh et al. 2024a; de
Ugarte Postigo et al. 2024a; Adami et al. 2024; Mohan
et al. 2024; Vinko et al. 2024; Dohyeong et al. 2024;
Moskvitin et al. 2024c; Pankov et al. 2024; Ror et al.
2024; Odeh et al. 2024b; Moskvitin et al. 2024b; Hu
et al. 2024; Moskvitin et al. 2024a; Lipunov et al. 2024;
Niwano et al. 2024; Rossi et al. 2024; An et al. 2024;
Martin et al. 2024).  It is noteworthy that an optical
transient was clearly detected during the prompt phase
by MASTER (Lipunov et al. 2024). The GTC telescope
reported a measurement of the redshift z = 2.695 (de
Ugarte Postigo et al. 2024a).
The radio detection of the afterglow was reported by
couple of  instruments approximately 5 days after the
burst, whose location is consistent with the optical af-
terglow position (Rhodes et al. 2024; de Ugarte Postigo
et al. 2024b).
+0.4
Given the fluence measurement of Sγ = 1.3−0.5 ×
−4  −2
10  erg cm  (20 keV − 10MeV) by Konus-Wind and
the redshift, the isotropic-equivalent gamma-ray energy
of the prompt emission, Eγ,iso is estimated to be Eγ,iso =
+0.7  54
2.2−0.8 ×10  erg (Svinkin et al. 2024), which makes the
GRB an energetic event among GRB population.
This means that GRB 240529A is a rare event with a
clear X-ray shallow decay feature, with Eγ,iso exceeding
54
10  erg. This is also illustrated by the right panel of
Figure 1, which shows the distribution of the temporal
index obtained from the light curve fit along with Eiso.
GRB 240529A possess the most shallow decay index of
0.2 in the sample, while other bursts show steeper de-
cay index than the typical value of 0.5, making GRB
240529A a distinct one among the sample. Due to its
unique characteristics, GRB 240529A may offer insights.

2.1. Fermi-LAT Analysis
The Fermi -LAT (Atwood et al. 2009) is sensitive to
gamma rays with energies from 100 MeV to 300 GeV
(Abdollahi et al. 2020) and has been continuously sur-
veying the entire sky every 3 hr since beginning opera-
5
tion in 2008 August. We use 6 × 10 s of data of Pass
8 SOURCE class data (Atwood et al. 2013; Bruel et al.
2018) between 100 MeV and 10 GeV. The upper bound
of the energy is chosen to be 10 GeV, since an absorp-
tion by extragalactic background light (EBL) starts to
be non-negligible in the higher energies. Events within
◦
20 of the GRB position (enhanced XRT position: R.A.,
◦  ◦
decl. = 335 . 358, +51 . 562) are extracted.  Events de-
◦
tected at zenith angles larger than 100 were excluded
to limit the contamination from gamma rays generated

We performed an unbinned likelihood analysis with
the  latest Fermitools package  (v2.2.0),  utilizing the
P8R3_SOURCE_V3 instrument response  function.  The
region of  interest  (ROI)  is modeled  from the  lat-
est Fermi -LAT source  catalog based on 14 yr  of
data, 4FGL (data release 4, Ballet et al.  (2020)) for
◦
point and extended sources  that are within 15  of
the ROI center, as well as the latest Galactic diffuse
and isotropic diffuse templates (gll_iem_v07.fits and
iso_P8R3_SOURCE_V3_v1.txt, respectively). Through-
out the analysis, the spectral parameters of the 4FGL
sources were kept fixed to the values from the catalog.
The background components were first determined us-
5
ing the data up to 6×10 s since T0. The obtained best-
fit spectral parameters of the background components
were fixed and used for the further analysis. Then, we
computed count and test statistic (TS) maps in order
to search for any residual gamma-ray emission. The TS
value is defined to be the natural logarithm of the ra-
tio of the likelihood of one hypothesis (e.g., presence of
one additional source) and the likelihood for the null
hypothesis (e.g., absence of source).  Note that there
3
were no gamma-ray events between T0 and t = 1×10 s
due to the ROI outside of LAT’s field of view,  and

4
hence the maps were generated with time intervals after
3
t = 1 × 10 s.  The computed TS maps are shown in
3  4
Figure 2. The first time interval (1 × 10 s–5 × 10 s) is
chosen to cover the X-ray shallow decay phase with a
sufficient margin of time. From the TS maps, we dis-
covered a point-like gamma-ray source coincident in po-
3
sition with GRB 240529A in the time range of 1×10 s–
4
5×10 s. This is the first GeV discovery for the shallow
54
decay GRBs with Eiso > 10  erg with a typical shallow
decay. No significant source is found at times beyond
4
t = 5 × 10 s.
The probability of each photon being associated by
GRB 240529A was  investigated.  We first performed
spectral likelihood analysis adopting PowerLaw2 model
with spectral  index fixed to 2.0, and then calculated
the probability using gtsrcprob. The top panel of Fig-
ure 3 shows the energy of each photon divided into dif-
ferent probability ranges. It can be seen that all events
with probability greater than 80% are included within
4
(1 − 3) × 10 s, overlapping the X-ray shallow decay
phase. Note that there is no event with probability be-
tween 80% and 90%, hence the probability greater than
80% is effectively greater than 90%.  The number of
events after T0 with probability greater than 90% (40%)
is 3 (12), while no events are detected with probability
greater than 40% for off-source positions offset from the
◦
GRB position by 5  (see Appendix A). Among the 3
events with probability greater than 90%, the highest
4
energy is 2.8 GeV, which was detected 3.2 × 10 s after
T0.  The bottom panel of Figure 3 shows the number
of detected photons together with the exposure.  The
4
number of photons increases during the first 3 × 10 s,
with no further increase observed afterward.  The cu-
mulative evolution in the figure shows that the number
of events with probability greater than 40% reaches 10
4
around 3 × 10 s after T0. The green markers represent
the exposure time, which illustrates the sufficient expo-
sure time for the time intervals with high-probability
photons. All the events with probability greater than
◦
90% are located within 0 .

2 from the GRB position.
We  localized the point  source with gtfindsrc to
find the best-fit position and uncertainty.  The  lo-
calized position for the new gamma-ray source is off-
◦
set by 0 . 05 from the exact position of GRB 240529A
◦  ◦
(R.A., decl. = 335 . 358, +51 . 562) and has R.A., decl. =
◦  ◦
335 . 43, +51 . 59 (J2000). The corresponding 95% posi-
◦
tional uncertainty radius is r = 0 . 18, indicating a good
consistency between the GRB position and the localized
position.
To model  the gamma-ray emission coincident with
GRB 240529A, we added a point source at the GRB
position to the source model. We set the spectrum to

PowerLaw2 model, with its photon index fixed to 2.0 or
allowed to vary. The derived photon flux (between 100
MeV and 10 GeV) is listed in Table 1. The returned
TS value for the case of the fixed photon index is 20,
corresponding to a detection significance of 4.5σ.
We further investigated the gamma-ray emission by
performing the time-resolved spectral analysis, utilizing
the same model function (similarly as stated above, the
photon index was either fixed to 2.0 or allowed to vary).
4
For t > 5×10 s, where no significant signal is observed,
95% confidence level flux upper limits are computed as-
suming a spectral index of 2.0 or 2.5. The results of the
spectral analysis are presented in Table 1. Despite the
obtained photon indices tend to show soft values (e.g.,
3  4
2.99 ± 0.64 in 1 × 10 s−1.6 × 10 s), the corresponding
errors are large thus best-fit values are compatible with
generic 2.0.
In order to check the stability of the results, analysis
is also performed using Multi-Mission Maximum Likeli-
hood 3ML (Vianello et al. 2015) and gtburst softwares.
3ML is only used for the spectral analysis of the LAT
data, whereas gtburst is used for the entire LAT analy-
sis, including the event selection up to the spectral anal-
ysis. The obtained flux values are consistent within the
1σ statistical uncertainties.
Following the procedure described in Fermi -LAT Sec-
ond Gamma-Ray Burst Catalog paper (Ajello et al.
2019), we calculated the duration of LAT emission and
fluence of GRB 240529A, and compared with the latest
1  4
LAT GRB catalog  . The duration is 2.2×10 s, which is
the third longest among the catalog. The 100 MeV–100
−5  −2  3  4
GeV fluence is 1.7×10  erg cm  (1×10 s−5×10 s;
index free), which is in the top 10% of the fluence values
for the extended emission. Despite of the weak signal,
the fluence resulted in a relatively high value due to the
long duration of the signal.
2.2.  Swift-XRT Analysis
Launched in November 2004,  the Swift  satellite  is
specifically designed for the study of GRBs (Gehrels
et al. 2004).  Swift-XRT is an X-ray imaging spec-
trometer with an energy range of 0.3–10 keV (Burrows
et al. 2005). The automated XRT light curves are ex-
2
tracted from the Burst Analyser website  (Evans, P. A.
et al. 2010). In order to check the temporal behavior of
GRB 240529A, we fit the 0.3–10 keV light curve with
a smoothly broken power-law (SBPL) function (Liang

The post-break decay index differs
from the typical value of the normal decay phase, which
is discussed later in Section 3.  The results for differ-
ent ω values are shown in Appendix B. As presented in

4
time tb = 1.92 × 10 s and the steeper post-decay index

4

4
In the later analysis, 1.6 × 10 s is chosen as the end
time of the shallow decay.  Figure 1 shows the XRT
0.3 − 10 keV light curve with the best-fit SBPL funtion.
As shown in Figure 1, the X-ray light curve in the
post-shallow decay phase shows no break that could be
associated with a jet break.  Therefore, we derived a
lower limit of the second break time by fitting the X-
ray light curve with a smooth triple power-law (STPL).

From Fermi -LAT spectral analysis, the flux ratio be-
tween the GeV and keV energy ranges is FGeV/FkeV =
4.2 ± 2.3,  strongly suggesting that the GeV emission
arises from an inverse Compton component rather than
a synchrotron component. This is also supported by the
photon index of 2.1 obtained from the XRT spectrum,
which is difficult to explain the obtained LAT flux by the
extrapolation of the XRT spectrum (see Figure 5). To
produce an efficient inverse Compton emission, a high
Compton parameter Y  is necessary,  indicating a low
energy fraction of the magnetic field ϵB, or high en-
ergy fraction of electrons ϵe and large blastwave energy
EK,iso.
To reproduce the  lightcurves and spectra,  primar-
ily focusing on the behavior during the shallow decay
phase, we adopt the simulation code in Asano (2024),
in which the shallow decay afterglow is discussed with
time-dependent simulations. The code was developed in
Fukushima et al. (2017) (see also, Asano et al. 2020).
The evolution of the electron and photon energy dis-
tributions in the shocked region is followed taking into

account particle injection,  synchrotron emission,  syn-
chrotron self-absorption,  inverse Compton (IC) emis-
sion,  γγ-absorption,  electron–positron pair  creation,
adiabatic cooling, and photon escape. The bulk Lorentz
factor Γ of the shocked region is self-consistently solved
with the energy conservation law. The photon flux for
observers is calculated considering the Doppler beaming
and the surface curvature. As shown in Asano (2024),
the photon spectra produced by this code are signif-
icantly different from the conventional analytical  for-
mulae (see e.g. Nakar et al. 2009; Yamasaki & Piran
2022),  in which the photon spectrum at an observer
time T is regulated by the local values of the circum-
stellar medium (CSM) density and Γ with microscopic
parameters (energy fraction of electrons ϵe, energy frac-
tion of the magnetic field ϵB, and the electron power-law
2
index p) at a given radius R ∝ Γ T . Our results depend
on the evolutionary history of the shock propagation.
Recent studies have shown that models with a single
emission zone and constant microscopic parameters have
difficulties reproducing multi-wavelength lightcurves in-
cluding gamma-ray ones. Two-component models (e,g.
Rhodes et al. 2022; Sato et al. 2023; Laskar et al. 2023;
Sun et al. 2024), whose parameter number is twice that
of  the single-component model, have been proposed.
Models with evolving microscopic parameters (e.g. Fof-
fano et al. 2024) are also possible, and we cannot deter-
mine which model is likely. Here, to discuss the mecha-
nism of the shallow decay phase and avoid complicated
model assumptions with many parameters, we focus on
mainly the early part of the afterglow with the simple
one-zone model. The later part of the afterglow, which
may require another component, jet break, or the evo-
lution of the model parameters,  is out of our scope in
this paper.
Here we adopt two models for the shallow decay phase:
the energy injection model (Zhang & M´esz´aros 2001;
Zhang et al. 2006; Granot & Kumar 2006) and the wind
model (Shen & Matzner 2011; Dereli-B´egu´e et al. 2022).
It should be mentioned that Sun et al. (2024) proposed a
model involving two shocks launched separately from the
central engine in order to explain the multiwavelength
4
emission of GRB 240529A  .
In energy injection model, while the forward shock
propagates in a CSM with a constant plasma density
−3
0.07 cm  , the isotropically-equivalent energy increases
4
From the results of Sun et al. (2024), the increase in energy in
the second shock can be interpreted as a type of energy injection
model, and the small initial Lorentz factor of the second shock is
similar to the wind model.

In wind model, the CSM density decreases with R as

where A∗ = 2.0, while the total energy E0 = 10  erg

−3
croscopic parameters are ϵe = 0.3, ϵB = 8 × 10  , and

Figure 4 and Figure 5 present reproduced multiwave-
length light curves and spectrum,  respectively, along
with observational data. Both models reproduce the X-
ray flux and spectrum during the shallow decay phase
4
(T−T0 : 600−1.6×10  s), with the X-ray flux consistent
5
within a factor of 2 .

Concerning the GeV light curve
during the shallow decay phase, we compared the mean
3  4
model flux, calculated between (T0+)1×10 −1.6×10 s,
with the LAT data. Both models yielded no significant
differences with respect to the LAT data, showing less
than 1 σ deviations.
In reproducing the GeV flux, the large ϵe and small
ϵB in the energy injection model are required to real-
ize the observed GeV flux by synchrotron self-Compton
p−1 (p+1)/4
(SSC) emission and the X-ray flux (∝ ϵe  ϵB  ) by
synchrotron emission keeping the synchrotron peak fre-
2 1/2
quency νm ∝ ϵeϵB  high enough to suppress the optical
flux.  In the energy injection model, the electron min-
imum Lorentz factor γm is much higher than that for
the wind model. Therefore, the spectral peak frequency
(∼ 0.1 TeV) of the SSC component is much higher than
that of the wind model (∼ 1 GeV). Therefore, the in-
trinsic TeV flux is suppressed in the wind model com-
pared to the energy injection model. Both models re-
55
quire EK,iso exceeding 10  erg, which implies a signifi-
cant amount of the blastwave kinetic energy, surpassing
5
Although the models deviate from the X-ray data especially in
the end of the shallow decay phase, the fine tuning of the model
parameters would not greatly impact the main consequences (the
interpretation of the GeV gamma-ray observations and the dis-
cussion on the jet energy) of this work.

the radiation energy released during the prompt phase
54
(2.2 × 10  erg).
As for GeV spectrum, the hard spectral index (∼ 1.24)
in the LAT energy range for the energy injection model
is deviated from the LAT result (2.99 ± 0.64) by 2.7σ,
while for the wind model case (index ∼ 1.91) the devia-
tion is 1.7σ. Thus, the LAT data shows a tension with
the energy injection model; however, the limited photon
statistics of the LAT data prevent a definitive conclu-
sion.  It should be noted that it is easier to reproduce
the soft GeV spectrum in the wind model due to its low
spectral peak frequency of the SSC component.
As demonstrated in Asano (2024), a precise measure-
ment of the GeV light curve and the spectral evolution
during the shallow decay phase is crucial to identify the
physical mechanism behind the shallow decay.  Since
the predicted TeV flux of the inverse Compton emission
differs by more than an order of magnitude between the
models, a precise measurement of TeV flux by upcoming
Cherenkov Telescope Array Observatory (CTAO) will be
able to constrain the shallow decay models. It should be
noted that for the energy injection model, CTAO can
well detect TeV emission from similar events even under
a strong EBL attenuation for a redshift z = 2.7 as shown
in Figure 5. This redshift value is significantly greater
than the highest redshift of z = 1.1 ever detected by
ground-based TeV instruments (Abe et al. 2023).
After the shallow decay phase, both the two models
5
deviate from the X-ray data for T − T0 > 10  s.  As
is well known, the optical lightcurve is the hardest one
to reproduce.  If the optical data should be treated as
upper limits, both the two models are inconsistent after

for the energy injection model case (left) and the wind model
green squares represent the LAT data, and the remaining colors
light curve is converted from 100 MeV – 10 GeV photon flux,
ing the photon index of 2.5 are also shown as the green open
Burst Analyser website. Note that the L band in the figures is
the shallow decay phase. It is challenging to explain all
the data,  including optical observations, with a single
jet model. Unlike Sun et al. (2024), our focus is primar-
4
ily on the behavior during the first few 10 s.  In fact,
Sun et al. (2024) note that the former shock has signifi-
cant uncertainties and cannot be thoroughly discussed,
so their work mainly concentrates on the later shock af-
4
ter few 10 s. This can be regarded as a complementary
approach to our work.
As the X-ray lightcurve shows a steeper decay than
usual, a potential explanation is the occurrence of a
jet break just after the shallow decay phase.  For the
electron injection index p = 2.8 inferred from our mod-
eling,  the temporal decay slope of 2.0 for the post-
shallow decay phase is steeper than the usual decay in-
dex 3(p−1)/4 = 1.35 and shallower than the slope after
the jet break p.

Although the modeling of the afterglow
after the shallow decay phase is out of scope in this pa-
per, a complicated model, such as the jet break with the
continuous energy injection or the parameter evolutions
(evolving p, etc.), may be required to reconcile with the
Fermi upper limit, optical data, and the steep decay of
the X-ray lightcurves. It should be noted that the 3 GeV
4
photon was triggered at t ∼ 3×10 s, which almost cor-
responds to the end of the X-ray shallow decay phase. A
rapid evolution of the parameters may be allowed after
this time.
Based on the X-ray light curve analysis and the mul-
tiwavelength modeling, we evaluate the jet opening an-
gle and hence the collimation-corrected jet energy. Sun
et al. (2024) calculated the isotropic X-ray luminosity
during the shallow decay phase and attributed it to.

X-ray light curves of gamma-ray bursts afterglow ex-
hibit a variety of features, with the shallow decay phase
being the most enigmatic.  Various models have been
proposed to explain the shallow decay phase, but the
physical mechanism of the shallow decay remains a mys-
tery.  Previous studies argue that hyper-energetic or
GeV–TeV detected GRBs display no or less prominent
shallow decay phase in their X-ray light curves (Ya-
mazaki et al. 2020; Sharma et al. 2021; Ding et al. 2022).
Recently discovered GRB 240529A has an extremely
54
large isotropic gamma-ray energy release of 2.2×10  erg
and a prominent X-ray shallow decay phase with a decay
index of 0.2. This indicates that the burst is a unique
case compared to previous studies and may provide a
valuable key to understanding the physical process of
the shallow decay phase. GeV–TeV observations will be
crucial for distinguishing between different models of the
shallow decay (Asano 2024).
In this work, we presented Fermi -LAT analysis of
GRB 240529A, indicating the evidence of GeV emission
(4.5σ) covering the X-ray shallow decay phase of the
GRB. This can be interpreted as the first case for hyper-
energetic GRBs with a typical shallow decay phase. The
flux level of the GeV emission is 4.2 ± 2.3 times larger
than the keV flux. Combined with the XRT spectral in-
dex of 2.1, this suggests that the GeV emission is dom-
inated by strong IC emission.  In order to discuss in a
multiwavelength context, we performed the multiwave-
length modeling based on the time-dependent simula-
tions and tested the two promising models of the shallow
decay phase:  the energy injection model and the wind

Figure 5. The multiwavelength SED at approximately 4000 s
energy injection model (solid line) and wind model (dashed lin
the optical data, and the green denotes the LAT differential flux
lines are model curves without EBL attenuation. The red line is
4
time of 10 s.
model.  In the modeling, small energy fraction of the
magnetic field, and large values of electron energy frac-
tion and blastwave energy are adopted for both models
to account for the efficient IC emission. As a result, the
55
blastwave energy exceeding 10  erg is required, which
implies a significant amount of the blastwave kinetic
energy, surpassing the radiation energy released during
the intense prompt phase. Together with XRT analysis
done in this work, the modeling results suggest that the
lower bound of the collimation-corrected total  jet en-
52
ergy is (1–2)×10  erg. This value is close to the max-
imum possible rotational energy of the magnetar, indi-
cating either a black hole or a magnetar with extremely
high jet energy conversion efficiency as the central en-
gine of GRB 240529A. Both models reproduce the X-ray
and GeV data during the shallow decay phase, though
the GeV spectrum indicates some tension with the en-
ergy injection model. Although distinguishing between
them requires better statistics, our modeling shows that
gamma-ray observations are a useful tool in constrain-
ing the shallow decay models. Future GeV−TeV obser-
vations by CTAO will play a pivotal role in constraining
models of the shallow decay phase.
